words: This conversation ith ELVON HAMPTON took place at his home in Genesee, Idaho
on May 3, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.
ELVON HAMPTON: Well, he came out at a fairly young age. I suppose he came out
probably- maybe twenty-six, something like that. Came to Dayton,
over there. It seems like a lot of the early pioneers
came to Dayton about that time and he worked there a while and he
told me about fishing up in those creeks up in there. And then for
some reason or other he moved further, out here, and there were
several other fellows with him from Carolina, and I know we used to
go to visit one, I forget his name now, up around east of Moscow.
And of course, in those days they wanted to homestead a little bit
up in the timber country. And they tried that, several of his friends
did, and probably established a homestead but he said for some reason
or other he didn't like that, it seemed kind of slow to him, sitting
out there and building a cabin and cutting timber and he just wasn't
quite as adapted to the timber life as some of these other men. And
so.he migrated I think right down here in this area and worked on a
farm, right just out here just a couple of miles by the highway that
come by, there used to be a^ out there and he talked about plo
wing with a footburner out there for a few years. And then he hap
pened to travel out east of Genesee again and then onto a place out
there that was for sale, by an insurance company. Seemed like in
those earlier days that insurance companies had possession of those
properties. And he said he bought that piece of ground right there
without even going back off of the road to look at it. He said the
land looked so good from the road and he just purchased that place
there then and started building- Oh, I guess there were some old buil
dings on it there- but he built up on it and improved on it. Then
he just kept gradually in those days, adding a little more to his
holdings there. He was next to the Indian land, the Nez Perce Re
servation down there and the Indians could sell their land then and
some of the Indians, you know,.- well, it wasn't too productive then
and they ran a few cattle on it and they weren't too successful. They
were willing to sell to somebody who would cultivate it. And was
n't anything like it is today because they had to operate it with horses
original and then later with mules that he used because
of hiring people, as I told you before.

speaker: SS
words: Let me ask you this: Did he ever tell you why he decided to leave

speaker: SS
words: Carolina and wanted to come West?

speaker: EH
words: No, I just can't recall, unless it was, I think his family wasn't too

speaker: EH
words: large, it wasn't on that basis, I suppose he just was ambitious and

speaker: EH
words: it seemed like back there they worked on a smaller scale. A he

speaker: EH
words: heard about this country, I don't know, but they raised tobacco back

speaker: EH
words: and manual labor,

speaker: EH
words: there and it was a hard task and a menial job, outside of that

speaker: EH
words: I just don't really know.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he probably had the idea of owning his owi spread?

speaker: EH
words: Well, I think so. I think that he was that type of a man. He played

speaker: EH
words: a banjo; he had a banjo, I know, and I kept that thing. He used to

speaker: EH
words: play, you know, rainy days. I never learned how to play that, but

speaker: EH
words: there's his old banjo. And it was a five string banjo. And I've

speaker: EH
words: been musical. I went to the singing route. And you can see where he

speaker: EH
words: just picked it- never did use a pick, I got this pick. But that is

speaker: EH
words: a five string banjo and he'd pick that. I remember, well, I guess

speaker: EH
words: before I knew about it he probably played out to dances and things

speaker: EH
words: like that. And whether he learned to play that back home, I don't

speaker: EH
words: know.

speaker: SS
words: Wouldn't surprise me because, because it is an instrument they played

speaker: SS
words: in the South more than around here.

speaker: EH
words: He could sit and play these old tunes, you know, Old Joe Clark, and

speaker: EH
words: I remember sitting around the coal stove at home up there at the

speaker: EH
words: ranch and when it was rainy days he'd get out this banjo and play it.

speaker: EH
words: And he loved that. And he always loved music. I took up the singing

speaker: EH
words: and I did a lot of singing and I still do, did that in my life. We

speaker: EH
words: fixed it up and that's the old banjo. It's an old instrument.

speaker: SS
words: About when do you think he landed here?

speaker: EH
words: Well, I think in about 1886 or '89, something like that.

speaker: SS
words: So there was no homestead land to be had around here, Genesee?

speaker: EH
words: No, it was up in the timber and you had to go down here and purchase

speaker: EH
words: it. When you acquired your property then)it was difficult to work

speaker: EH
words: it, because you had to travel and we didn't have the mobility that

speaker: EH
words: we have today. It's hard to visualize that. We hauled our grain

speaker: EH
words: with wagons to town. And in my time, it amazes me that I can think

speaker: EH
words: back that we did that and we traveled by sled in those early days.

speaker: EH
words: I never got to town, like now, unless we got to town, you know, on

speaker: EH
words: the Fourth of July or some special time. Hardly ever went out for

speaker: EH
words: lunch anyplace. That would be something.

speaker: SS
words: Why would that be?

speaker: EH
words: Well, we was so busy then, those days, unless you went for a purpose

speaker: EH
words: then. If you took your surrey and your team you went to town to

speaker: EH
words: buy groceries or else in the fall you took your wheat to grind it

speaker: EH
words: a flour mill And so in those days, you went for a purpose.

speaker: EH
words: You didn't say, "We'll just go in and get the mail." Or,V'We're

speaker: EH
words: going in and get a quart of milk." You didn't have to get milk then,

speaker: EH
words: because you had milk cows. I milked a cow even after I was married.

speaker: EH
words: And we butchered our hogs, our pork. We had our bacon and cured all

speaker: EH
words: of that. Made our lard and made the soap. There was a lady down here,

speaker: EH
words: Mrs. Crane, used to come out; and I can see her in the yard yet, we

speaker: EH
words: put a forked holder up and put thft big round pot in that to boil that

speaker: EH
words: lard, stir it; stir it, you know, and it'd harden.

speaker: SS
words: She would come over to make the soap?

speaker: EH
words: Yeah, she was a woman that lived in town here, a German lady, and

speaker: EH
words: she knew how to do that. And so we'd always get her to come out and

speaker: EH
words: she would take charge of that. She was a specialist, you might say.

speaker: EH
words: And we'd cut this meat up, you know, and had no refrigeration and no

speaker: EH
words: place in town, you had to cure it and can your meat. And then in the

speaker: EH
words: summertime when we needed meat, we'd kill a lamb, we had sheep, kill

speaker: EH
words: lamb whenever we needed some fresh to eat. We'd maybe kill a beef

speaker: EH
words: that would cure out in the cool of night, you know. Of course, the

speaker: EH
words: hog, we butchered in the wintertime when it was cold. Used to butcher

speaker: EH
words: a dozen or more hogs. Of course, we had men, in the summertime, quite

speaker: EH
words: a few men and it took a lot of food. Baking; baked all our bread.

speaker: EH
words: When I talk about that, I think a fellow- the guy's talking

speaker: EH
words: through his hat! The difference. I've often recall, how I think

speaker: EH
words: back how this change has come. I told you about something the last

speaker: EH
words: time you was here; there's a picture of those mules and the combine.

speaker: EH
words: That's in '31. Well, '31, that was two years before I graduated out

speaker: EH
words: of college, and I was driving a team there, and then you see how we

speaker: EH
words: progressed when we had this, and then this one back here, you see,

speaker: EH
words: we started with the tractor. Had a tractor; that was a smaller one

speaker: EH
words: and we just experimented with that. And we eventually, here a little

speaker: EH
words: later, the last year after this one, I think in '33, why we used six

speaker: EH
words: mules back here and we put this tractor up in front and we cut off

speaker: EH
words: all these mule, trying to get changed over to a mechanized system.

speaker: SS
words: Would you explain to me again why it was that your father preferred

speaker: SS
words: the mules to the horses. He was in the minority of opinion down

speaker: SS
words: around this country.

speaker: EH
words: Yes, I don't suppose there were too many. I can't think of too many

speaker: EH
words: more, except there used to be a fellow up around Moscow, Boyds, were

speaker: EH
words: big operators; generally had to be bigger operators. And he did that

speaker: EH
words: because, I think- I have some pictures of horses down there- that

speaker: EH
words: the casualty rate in horses was pretty high, because they're more of

speaker: EH
words: a personalized animal. You had to look after 'em, you had to not get

speaker: EH
words: 'em overheated or watch their food; for instance they'll founder,

speaker: EH
words: whereas this mule won't do that. He'll take care of himself. If

speaker: EH
words: he's too hot when he comes in, and you've been working him hard in

speaker: EH
words: arm.

speaker: EH
words: the field, he won t water. And when you take him up to the

speaker: EH
words: barn he won't- you never hardly find a mule that'll founder. He won't

speaker: EH
words: just jump in and eat all that grain that he's got there. He'll ra

speaker: EH
words: tion himself.

speaker: SS
words: Did this have to do with his thinking that with a hired men it would

speaker: SS
words: be easier to look after mules?

speaker: EH
words: Yeah. You know, he just be out there every day to caution

speaker: EH
words: the men. He'd hire a man and say, "There's your six or seven mules,

speaker: EH
words: you go up there and harness them." And the grain, we'd have the bulk

speaker: EH
words: grain in the bin, "And go feed them, you know." You know how a man

speaker: EH
words: would come in, if he was a careless man, he would come in and take

speaker: EH
words: a bucket and maybe dump a couple of gallons instead of one gallon

speaker: EH
words: in one box and maybe not so much in the others. Well, if you did

speaker: EH
words: that to a mule he wouldn t be sick the next morning. And that

speaker: EH
words: happened and when the men went up there to work he'd probably say,

speaker: EH
words: "Well, here, I got a sick horse." And that probably held up his

speaker: EH
words: unit all day. And it was quite aproblem. And not only horses

speaker: EH
words: they were temperamental. Iremember him telling about anice,

speaker: EH
words: big horses and they would come in here during the week, and they're

speaker: EH
words: balky. You get anice, big horse, you know, they call a cold

speaker: EH
words: shouldered " horse and he just wouldn't hit that collar. And the

speaker: EH
words: fellow come in and he'd trade that horse off and you'd think

speaker: EH
words: he was anice one, well, the next morning, or the next Monday morning

speaker: EH
words: hooked him up to leave town or something and they had a road, that

speaker: EH
words: horse wouldn't pull.

speaker: SS
words: What's "cold shouldered"?

speaker: EH
words: Well, he just wouldn't hit that collar, you know. Put abig old

speaker: EH
words: collar on him and he was cold shouldered, he just wouldn't hit it

speaker: EH
words: he'd just back up, just sit there a little bit and just back right

speaker: EH
words: up in the traces. I've seen 'em do that great, big horses. They re

speaker: EH
words: just what you'd call- they're balky.

speaker: SS
words: So if you would trade for a horse, you were taking chances.

speaker: SS
words: So lots of time my dad wouldtome in, he'd get stuck, you know, and

speaker: SS
words: he'd have to wait till the next week,Ahe'd have to substitute some

speaker: SS
words: way, he'd bring this nice, pretty horse back and fellows would come

speaker: SS
words: by and they'd probably be in the same spot that he was, nobody knew

speaker: SS
words: what was the matter, so they'd trade horses. That's what they call

speaker: SS
words: horse WCfS That's where horse got the ame. And they

speaker: SS
words: would look around and trade, and he'd come back smiling and say,

speaker: SS
words: "When that feller he's going to be

speaker: SS
words: Do you think this had something to do with why he preferred mules?

speaker: SS
words: And Yeah, that's right, they were more reliable. And they had a man

speaker: SS
words: to work a team and he couldn't go buy a horse or replace him;Agenerally

speaker: SS
words: had two or three mules who were extras, you know, get hurt

speaker: SS
words: or something else. And another thing, they're not a nervous type

speaker: SS
words: of animal. If they ever get in a wire fence they'll stand there

speaker: SS
words: you come and get 'em, and a horse'11 just tear himself apart. Seen

speaker: SS
words: a horse get in there and just get excited and just cut his legs off,

speaker: SS
words: on a barbwire fence. So you can see the advantage of that kind of

speaker: SS
words: animal.

speaker: SS
words: I take it that he started with horses and decided to turn to mules.

speaker: EH
words: Yeah, I remember when they had horses, I could show you a picture

speaker: EH
words: of that- all those horses lined out. But he switched pretty early

speaker: EH
words: in my life, I was a young boy then.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know where he went for his mules?

speaker: EH
words: I remember he would go- you see, you have to breed those, they won't

speaker: EH
words: reproduce; mules won't reproduce, have to breed a jack with a

speaker: EH
words: horse, and there was fellows that would raise . Like I said,

speaker: EH
words: this Boyd up here, they did that and they kept a bunch of brood mares

speaker: EH
words: I remember one time when he went to Montana or someplace and We

speaker: EH
words: bought a bunch of yearling to two year old pretty mules and my dad

speaker: EH
words: left me there with another hired man to tie these up, you know. And

speaker: EH
words: they was pretty, they had some bay ones, they were different colored

speaker: EH
words: you know, generally they're brown, but you see there's some white

speaker: EH
words: ones there. And they were pretty fellows and we had a lot of fun

speaker: EH
words: tying those things up.

speaker: SS
words: Did he get ribbed about having mules instead of horses by the guys

speaker: SS
words: around here. It's so unusual.

speaker: EH
words: No. No, I don't think anybody ever did that, because they knew the

speaker: EH
words: advantage of it. But, you know, you take a fellow, if he was an

speaker: EH
words: individual farmer, he took pride in his horses and watched 'em and

speaker: EH
words: took care of 'em. He could get by a lot better, you know, because

speaker: EH
words: he knew just how much to feed 'em- any particular horse- but where

speaker: EH
words: you had five or six fellows working, you know, maybe one man would

speaker: EH
words: leave you for somebody else and you had to break him in. Of course,

speaker: EH
words: it didn't change too much in those days. If a man was a mule skinner,

speaker: EH
words: what they call a mule skinner, why, he'd stay for maybe year after

speaker: EH
words: year.

speaker: SS
words: I'd like to talk about that some. But I'd like to talk about the

speaker: SS
words: operation, itself. First, maybe you'd tell me how he went from

speaker: SS
words: being a small farmer to a pretty good sized operator.

speaker: EH
words: (Pictures) Here's my dad standing on a stackpile. See, that's when

speaker: EH
words: he used to have those threshing machines. Now here's one out at

speaker: EH
words: the place where they had these mules, see, they'd all come in here

speaker: EH
words: to drink, and here's the fellows that's lined up there working on

speaker: EH
words: his place. And here has to be a picture of the crew that summer,

speaker: EH
words: shows you there. There is my dad, there I am and my brother and

speaker: EH
words: here's a feller that worked for many, many years. And here s what

speaker: EH
words: I say when they had these horses, they'd have colts and stuff, and

speaker: EH
words: that was in 1904. I think there were six or seven^team, six, I guess.

speaker: EH
words: And here's my homestead originally where I lived and I was born in

speaker: EH
words: that house. Here's a picture of a combine we took when we had, later

speaker: EH
words: on with the tractor.

speaker: SS
words: What was the means by which he built up his place?

speaker: EH
words: He was quite a trader. He raised livestock. He would have some

speaker: EH
words: sheep; we had quite a few sheep, hogs and everything, you know, self sufficient,

speaker: EH
words: you know. And grain, as you know, wasn't too productive

speaker: EH
words: a thing, the yield wasn't too high then. And he was a good manager.

speaker: EH
words: Whatever the land produced, he protected that and was able to come out

speaker: EH
words: on it. We didn't lay away a lot of money. And of course property

speaker: EH
words: wasn't as high then, you didn't have a big cash flow. And didn't

speaker: EH
words: buy gasoline or stuff like that. You just put it into what little

speaker: EH
words: labor you had then. And lots of times he would provide you know-

speaker: EH
words: I've known some fellows that would just come out there and work for

speaker: EH
words: their board; chop wood for the wintertime, maybe and keep the place

speaker: EH
words: together while we were in school.

speaker: SS
words: Did he save up enough money to put it down on more land and buy more?

speaker: SS
words: Did he expand?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, yeah, he would save a little and he would always borrow at the

speaker: EH
words: bank down here. There was a fellow name of Breshler, who was a good

speaker: EH
words: banker and he would stay with him, and he would loan him the money,

speaker: EH
words: lots of times pretty near all of it. And maybe he'd have a crop of

speaker: EH
words: wheat or barley- and like I say- the cash flow wasn't very great

speaker: EH
words: and expense was great, you had a lot of labor and you had to han

speaker: EH
words: dle it with sacks and sew 'em and haul 'em. It was slow, but what

speaker: EH
words: you did was comparable, more so than it is today, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Did he mind being in debt and being extended?

speaker: EH
words: No, I guess he saw the opportunity was great and people weren't too

speaker: EH
words: anxious to- it was hard to have a crew and horses and stuff like

speaker: EH
words: that if you're willing to go on, and opportunity was there, you know.

speaker: EH
words: I know that in those days you had more land than you wanted. You j ft

speaker: EH
words: had more land than you wanted just scattered around; nobody seemed

speaker: EH
words: to want it.

speaker: SS
words: What's the Driscoll part in the partnership?

speaker: EH
words: He was a man here that had been a schoolteacher and was a slight type

speaker: EH
words: of a man, not a farmer type, but a financier. And he ran a hardware

speaker: EH
words: store down here, and he saved his money and looked around and he went

speaker: EH
words: in partnership; my dad helped to finance him. And so Dad did the

speaker: EH
words: operation and he paid him a salary and he made the living. Even in

speaker: EH
words: that early day, he did it from a financial standpoint. And so my

speaker: EH
words: dad knew the farming end of it so they formed a partnership, just

speaker: EH
words: like lots of times you form a partnership.

speaker: SS
words: Did this mean they had separate land holdings at the same time? Your

speaker: SS
words: father had his own land-

speaker: EH
words: When he acquired land- like that team was- that was on his place

speaker: EH
words: there, you see- why, he would buy a piece of land- he bought a piece

speaker: EH
words: of land, what they called the Troutman place, and from then on they

speaker: EH
words: aded each one; he had his place over where this homestead was, see,

speaker: EH
words: and he lived there. It's about a mile from there. And then when

speaker: EH
words: property would come up, why, they would buy- take maybe a half of

speaker: EH
words: an eighty that laid next to them, next to their property, which would

speaker: EH
words: be more convenient, why, they'd buy it that way. And when Indian

speaker: EH
words: land came up my dad more or less bought that down in the Nez Perce

speaker: EH
words: area. I don't know of Driscoll ever buying any of that. Why he

speaker: EH
words: didn't, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: So once he got going, how much land was he actually farming?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, when he was farming there they had around, just about 2,000 acres.

speaker: SS
words: About where would this have been?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, that would have been in the '30's- well, up to the '30's- '35.

speaker: EH
words: And then when Driscoll died- he died first- his daughter went in

speaker: EH
words: partnership. They had a partnership and I a partnership with

speaker: EH
words: her and we farmed for quite a few years.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have much of an idea of how many years it took your father

speaker: SS
words: to be farming that much? He probably started with what? 160 acres?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, well, I suppose that they bought- I suppose he started

speaker: EH
words: in about 1895 and it took him thirty-forty years.

speaker: SS
words: To build up to that? That 2,000.

speaker: EH
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: But they were doing some kind of partnership by 1900, already. Be

speaker: SS
words: cause they had the threshing crews; joint crew.

speaker: EH
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: So your father, from what you were mentioning before, he looked at

speaker: SS
words: himself as a manager. That's what he wanted to do.

speaker: EH
words: Yeah, there's a picture of him up there. Very stern. I never can

speaker: EH
words: think of him driving a team. He was always the man that was out mana

speaker: EH
words: ging.

speaker: EH
words: MRS. H: Tell him about the ? idea when he showed you how to drive.

speaker: EH
words: One time when I was young and we had these push binders. You drive

speaker: EH
words: with six mules and there's a beam and it more or less came closer

speaker: EH
words: to what these combines are now, you know, because the binder would

speaker: EH
words: go ahead of your team and the reel would cut that, although the bullwheel

speaker: EH
words: would knock down your wheat. And so I was a young fella then

speaker: EH
words: and I remember starting up the road with this team, and if you had

speaker: EH
words: a green team it was. hard to hold them in a little bit and you had

speaker: EH
words: a rudder, it reminds me of these outboard motors, you could just

speaker: EH
words: swing around, but this rudder had a seat on it with a wooden peg

speaker: EH
words: in between it, you'd guide it like this, just swing, like this, you

speaker: EH
words: know. I think you understand what I mean. I don't have a picture

speaker: EH
words: of one of those. And so my dad was along the road one day and I

speaker: EH
words: was coming along and I was diving in and out like this trying to keep

speaker: EH
words: my team in and one thing and another, and he was telling me what to

speaker: EH
words: do; a very patient fellow, though. And finally I jumped off and I

speaker: EH
words: said, "You take it." And he said, "No, Son, I don't know how to do

speaker: EH
words: it but I know how it should be done." So I got back on that and

speaker: EH
words: I stayed with it. I thought that was good enough for me! Course,

speaker: EH
words: he had done some earlier work when he was a young fellow, Dad worked

speaker: EH
words: hard. But he was a good manager. Good to figure. He never threw

speaker: EH
words: anything away. Treat his employees as good as he could. And he

speaker: EH
words: was always a fair man.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of dimensions, as far as the size of the crew and the

speaker: SS
words: amount of stock, did he need to farm 2,000 acres?

speaker: EH
words: Well, he just probably needed- there's lots of times he wouldn't get

speaker: EH
words: it plowed or done and the pressure is great. I remember even in a

speaker: EH
words: partnership to produce. This Driscoll was a fellow who could come

speaker: EH
words: out and take a look take a drive around and inspect and see

speaker: EH
words: well, have you got it seeded right. He wouldn't know how to seed

speaker: EH
words: but he would know the end result.

speaker: SS
words: By the size of the wheat growing in the field.

speaker: EH
words: Yes. I know that one time- he would come out- and it would make

speaker: EH
words: you kind of nervous after you'd been working and I remember my dad

speaker: EH
words: said that he came out and looked at the crop and he says, "It isn't

speaker: EH
words: going to make very much." I don't know whether he said twenty bushel,

speaker: EH
words: or something, and my dad said, "I'll buy your share out of that."

speaker: EH

words: So there was really a pretty strong line between the people living

speaker: SS
words: in the country and the town?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, yeah, in here, you know, you were just right here, that's all

speaker: EH
words: there is to it. Maybe there'd be a dozen people- course, they had

speaker: EH
words: the lodge here and the churches, but I just don't remember very much

speaker: EH
words: about it.

speaker: SS
words: Your own thinking about farming, is another thing I want to ask you

speaker: SS
words: about. You hadn't really planned on it particularly on going into

speaker: SS
words: farming when you were growing up, is what you said to me.

speaker: EH
words: When I went to school I started out in business and then I switched

speaker: EH
words: over; and I know I'd always had a ye^n for music and I always liked

speaker: EH
words: music and I did quite a little bit of singing locally. There was

speaker: EH
words: a lady here that was interested in opera singing and she taught quite

speaker: EH
words: a bit in that field. And so after I got out of school, although I

speaker: EH
words: did finish in agriculture, but I took a lot of music. I took some

speaker: EH
words: diction and some foreign languages and I studied music all the time,

speaker: EH
words: took private lessons and sang. And in fact, I gave a recital up

speaker: EH
words: there in my graduation in vocal. So when I started out, finished

speaker: EH
words: school I kind of thought well, I kind of want to follow that. And

speaker: EH
words: I went down to Frisco and tried out in a few places and sang in some

speaker: EH
words: night clubs, and finally decided that wasn't for me, and so I came

speaker: EH
words: back to the farm.

speaker: SS
words: What made you decide that that wasn't what you wanted?

speaker: EH
words: Well, it was night life; you know, you'd go into these places and

speaker: EH
words: it was at night and I wasn't used to that kind of a life. I didn't

speaker: EH
words: realize that was the way- when I was having a lot of fun and wasn't

speaker: EH
words: working for any money, I thought that was really fun, but when it

speaker: EH
words: got serious that made me make up my mind pretty fast. That night

speaker: EH
words: life and sleeping in the morning, all day, and associating with

speaker: EH
words: guys that sleep all day, just didn't suit me.

speaker: SS
words: This was in the early '30's?

speaker: EH
words: Yeah, that was in the early '30's- was '33.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of people were going to the night clubs in San Francisco

speaker: SS
words: in those days? Was it very popular?

speaker: EH
words: They had these piano bars, you know and just a small type of-

speaker: SS
words: So people were drinking again for the first time?

speaker: EH
words: It was pretty rough. Then there was a fellow- I went back as a dele

speaker: EH
words: gate for my fraternity back to Mackinaw Island, Michigan, Sigma Nu

speaker: EH
words: Fraternity, and I sang back there. And they got a great, big resort

speaker: EH
words: hotel there and I was at the national convention and I sang back there.

speaker: EH
words: And there was a fellow heard me and he thought I had a great possibility

speaker: EH
words: and so he said, "You come back to 'Frisco and I'll get you

speaker: EH
words: some interviews there." And I did, but I wasn't too successful. I

speaker: EH
words: had some interviews and I waited around and waited around and I

speaker: EH
words: didn't persevere or nothing and I " I didn't quite have the

speaker: EH
words: background that I should have had, and I didn't persevere or nothing.

speaker: EH
words: And I didn't have quite the background that I should have had.

speaker: SS
words: Were you interested yourself in popular singing?

speaker: EH
words: Yeah, I was interested in that.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get much of a background at the University for popular singing?

speaker: EH
words: No, I had to develop myself, because I had a who wanted me

speaker: EH
words: to sing opera and stuff like that and he would give me some of that

speaker: EH
words: stuff and I'd go down to the Chamber of Commerce and pick out some

speaker: EH
words: of the lighter stuff. So we clashed on that basis. He was always

speaker: EH
words: trying to keep me up under the heavier classical stuff and I was

speaker: EH
words: just practical enough that I knew the people didn't like that, so

speaker: EH
words: when he'd me out to sing a song I'd pick another one.

speaker: SS
words: What opportunities did you have for singing?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, we'd go down and I'd sing in front of the Chamber of Commerce

speaker: EH
words: there in Moscow and I joined the choir, the Lutheran Choir, I sang

speaker: EH
words: in the Lutheran Choir. We went to Spokane and sang over the radio

speaker: EH
words: and we took a tour and went down to Olympia, Washington and down the

speaker: EH
words: Coast and stuff like that. (Here's my banjo)

speaker: EH
words: I suppose I'd better get out to the ranch.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, okay. Could we take about another five, ten minutes? Because

speaker: SS
words: there's one more thing I want to ask you about: How some of the early

speaker: SS
words: changes, patterns in farming changed, in the way that you mentioned

speaker: SS
words: to me some of these innovations at the time you would see them be

speaker: SS
words: ginning and they would spread; and I'm wondering if you can describe

speaker: SS
words: a few of those changes in farming. There was a couple of things about

speaker: SS
words: when peas- there was one invention-

speaker: EH
words: I always attribute this area to be very aggressive around here be

speaker: EH
words: cause we live next to the University. I took advantage of their ex

speaker: EH
words: periments and new strains of grain. And of course, being as close

speaker: EH
words: as we are here, we took Advantage of the county agents and we were

speaker: EH
words: a productive land here. I just think myself, that we progressed.

speaker: EH
words: Like you mentioned when the peas started in here as a crop,

speaker: EH
words: we had a neighbor out there who was an inventor and we did a lot of

speaker: EH
words: experimenting around here with equipment. In latere after the experi

speaker: EH
words: menting that the old line companies took up, a lot of the patents

speaker: EH
words: hero

speaker: EH
words: that the farmers had worked , old John Deere done quite a bit

speaker: EH
words: of that and they recognized the ability of the men around here.

speaker: SS
words: Who was your neighbor who was the inventor?

speaker: EH
words: Man by the name of Charles Schooler he lived next to us. And

speaker: EH
words: he was the type of feller that liked to work at working out some

speaker: EH
words: thing like that and he worked out a pickup bar on the front of a

speaker: EH
words: combine, one of the first, before Hume-Love ever developed that

speaker: EH
words: up here in Garfield and made a national thing out of it. But he

speaker: EH
words: worked out this pickup bar that we used for several years out there,

speaker: EH
words: in these peas. And we would mow them and windrow them and save this

speaker: EH
words: picking them up and putting them in a wagon and hauling them into

speaker: EH
words: the threshing machine and this thing would just pick 'em up without

speaker: EH
words: a reel and put 'em into this combine. And that was quite an inven

speaker: EH
words: tion at that time. And that was the beginning of the harvesting

speaker: EH
words: of those peas.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like you're saying that a lot of the innovation came from the

speaker: SS
words: farmers themselves.

speaker: EH
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Not just from the University.

speaker: EH
words: No. The University didn t bring on th«Jmechanical things. They

speaker: EH
words: brought the rotations of the crops and the seed improvements and

speaker: EH
words: those things. But the mechanical innovations, they originated from

speaker: EH
words: the farmers. And even they developed a beater, what they call a Mos

speaker: EH
words: cow beater they could put in these- you know when they developed

speaker: EH
words: the self-propelling combine, that they were so steep in there that

speaker: EH
words: the grain wouldn't move up out of there, and they developed a beater

speaker: EH
words: in there, what they call a Moscow beater, a mechanic up there, and

speaker: EH
words: they used that very widely for a long time.

speaker: SS
words: What did the beater do?

speaker: EH
words: Well, it helped move this grain, straw, up in there instead of bac

speaker: EH
words: king down into the cylinder and that moved it out of the combine.

speaker: EH
words: And some way they just didn't have that developed enough to do that

speaker: EH
words: lipoid kind of a job. You go down a hill and it so steep there, that

speaker: EH
words: you couldn't keep that up, just keep rolling back and had to put

speaker: EH
words: this extra piece of machinery in there. And actually when they sold

speaker: EH
words: these combines- we bought several- why you had to go and buy that

speaker: EH
words: separately and put it in there. had a patent on it in Mos-

speaker: EH
words: cow. Fellow name of Crowe.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever try the Idaho Harvester, out of Moscow?

speaker: EH
words: No. I remember a fellow out here had one out here, Hanson, out here

speaker: EH
words: where John is now, and that's about the only one I know

speaker: EH
words: of- ever knew of around here, and that was that Moscow Harvester.

speaker: EH
words: They had a plant right there where Rosauer's is, in that area in there,

speaker: EH
words: In fact, I used to take my agricultural engineering in those plants

speaker: EH
words: there. After they were dismantled and they quit manufacturing, we

speaker: EH
words: used to go down there, we didn't have buildings up on the campus and

speaker: EH
words: we d have to go down there and have.classes.

speaker: SS
words: I guess what I'm thinking of; overall how much do you think the peo

speaker: SS
words: ple around here, the farmers, were thinking about always improving

speaker: SS
words: the methods. Was that a real active concern that a lot of farmers

speaker: SS
words: were thinking about? Or would it be the rare person, like this one

speaker: SS
words: inventor, be thinking about how to do things better?

speaker: EH
words: Well, no, I think there was an incentive for them to do it because

speaker: EH
words: everybody didn't have the ability to do that. In fact, I had another

speaker: EH
words: neighbor there before the: self-propelled combines came on that we had

speaker: EH
words: these pulled combines, like you saw here, that he and this same

speaker: EH
words: feller, Charlie Schooler got together and changed one of these

speaker: EH
words: machines over to be a self-propelled. They put a chain on it, put

speaker: EH
words: another motor on it and they used it for a year or two but it was

speaker: EH
words: the beginning of something that was better. And it never developed

speaker: EH
words: but they were trying to make some innovations, to make this machine

speaker: EH
words: work that way. And it did work for a fashion, but not too good.

speaker: SS
words: How much do you think that this area has characteristics for farming

speaker: SS
words: conditions that were unique? It was shared by other parts of the

speaker: SS
words: Palouse, too, but do you think of this being kind of a different

speaker: SS
words: area from a lot of wheat farming that's done?

speaker: EH
words: I just couldn't rightfully say what motivated that. But I think

speaker: EH
words: that the farmers- you look back over the period, saw the Jaeed, you

speaker: EH
words: know. You had to do things the slow way. You had to do things the

speaker: EH
words: hard ray. There was a waste to it. And you thought, "Well, here's

speaker: EH
words: an improvement. We can eliminate some of these things that I men

speaker: EH
words: tioned. We'll make it a little easier." And you could see that we

speaker: EH
words: were bending toward the mechanized system. And now you look back

speaker: EH
words: the years, and you can see that- Like I say, we started with

speaker: EH
words: those mules and then we started with another combine and then we had

speaker: EH
words: mules and a tractor, which was a similar program there with power.

speaker: EH
words: I ran across a fellow- was it down here in Portland the other day?

speaker: EH
words: I was getting some bark for my son and there was an older fellow

speaker: EH
words: there,Nquite a bit older than I was, and I asked him where he was.

speaker: EH
words: "Well," he said, "I lived up in Canada." AndAthey were pushing this

speaker: EH
words: bark around with the tractors. "You know," he says, "I drove one of

speaker: EH
words: those machines with a front wheel on it and I had a track type

speaker: EH
words: on it in my early days." And I said, "I know, we had one on our

speaker: EH
words: place like that." And it was very crude, but it was a big, heavy

speaker: EH
words: two

speaker: EH
words: old ohing, you know and it had an iron wheel in front and track

speaker: EH
words: types in the back. But the Holt Company made those. Didn't stay

speaker: EH
words: around here very long. Couldn't get it up in the hills, turn

speaker: EH
words: around the corners, but it was a beginning.

speaker: SS
words: Did you learn much in agricultural engineering at the University

speaker: SS
words: that turned out to be helpful to you when you started farming here?

speaker: EH
words: No, I wouldn't say there was. more or less oriented you towards

speaker: EH
words: the program and probably instigated you to seek better methods, but

speaker: EH
words: as far as just actually improving the mechanization per se, I wouldn't

speaker: EH
words: say that it did. Because I don't think at those earlier times that

speaker: EH
words: we had progressed to the point where you had the research in engin

speaker: EH
words: eering that you have now. We were just feeling around for those

speaker: EH
words: kind of things.

speaker: SS
words: So most of what you learned then, maybe you learned here from the

speaker: SS
words: farming here, not in the classroom?

speaker: EH
words: I just wouldn't discount the educational background, it gives you

speaker: EH
words: a background. It gives you an opportunity to ad lib, you might say.

speaker: EH
words: But it's hard to beat the practical standpoint. But I think you

speaker: EH
words: need both of 'em to progress very far.

speaker: SS
words: Did you take over from your father soon after you got back here,

speaker: SS
words: after college? I mean, when you started in?

speaker: EH
words: Yes. I started in. He gave me a piece of ground myself and so I op

speaker: EH
words: erated independently for a while until he quit farming and then I

speaker: EH
words: took over the operation of the larger farm.

speaker: SS
words: About when?

speaker: EH
words: Oh, let's see, it would probably been about '38.
